coredata.dc:title: Spray-assisted deposition of CsPbBr3 films in ambient air for large-area inorganic perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Jialong
Dou, Dawei
Zhao, Yuanyuan
Wang, Yudi
Yang, Xiya
Yuan, Haiwen
He, Benlin
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: High-purity cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr3) perovskite films are expected to promote the commercial manufacture of inorganic perovskite solar cells (PSCs). We present here a spray-assisted deposition of high-quality CsPbBr3 films in ambient air by spin-coating a PbBr2 layer and sequentially spraying CsBr multilayer to simplify the film fabrication technology. Upon optimizing spray deposition at four cycles, a high-purity CsPbBr3 phase is prepared and a maximized power conversion efficiency of 5.68% is achieved for the carbon-based inorganic PSC device. Furthermore, molybdenum disulfide quantum dots with an intermediate energy level are set at CsPbBr3/carbon interface to markedly increase charge extraction and to suppress electron-hole recombination, arising from the perovskite grain boundary passivation. Finally, the optimal inorganic PSC yields a stabilized efficiency of 6.80% under one sun illumination. Using this spray-assisted deposition method, this CsPbBr3 solar cell with an active area of 1 cm2 achieves an efficiency of 4.12%. The photovoltaic performances are relatively stable even upon 80% relative humidity over 700 h without encapsulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inorganic perovskite solar cells
Cesium lead bromide perovskite film
Interfacial modification
Spray-assisted deposition
Grain boundary passivation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustrated diagrams for making inorganic PSCs as well as top-view SEM images of different films. The scale bar represents 1 μm. (b) Top-view SEM photograph of MoS2 QDs passivated CsPbBr3 grain boundaries and (c) cross-sectional SEM image of a total inorganic PSC device. (d) Schematic diagrams of an inorganic PSC and (e) charge transportation processes.

(a) Total and (b) magnified XRD patterns for the FTO/TiO2/perovskite photoanodes synthesized by the spray-assisted deposition. (c) Phase transformation from PbBr2-rich CsPb2Br5 to perovskite CsPbBr3 and to CsBr-rich Cs4PbBr6 at different CsBr deposition cycles.

(a) J–V curves of the all-inorganic PSCs based on spraying-assisted deposition, and (b) IPCE spectra and integrated current densities of the PSCs with and without MoS2 QDs. (c) PL emission spectra and (d) time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of CsPbBr3 film with and without MoS2 QDs. Plots of (e) J sc and (f) V oc as a function of light intensity for the optimized devices.
(a) J–V plots of all-inorganic PSCs with different sizes. (b) The plots of PCEs versus active area. (c) The images of the PSC devices with different areas.

The photovoltaic parameters of all-inorganic PSCs with and without MoS2 QDs at different n cycles. PCE: power conversion efficiency; J sc: short-circuit current density; V oc: open-circuit voltage; FF: fill factor.
The photovoltaic parameters of all-inorganic PSCs with different sizes.

head.ce:title: Spray-assisted deposition of CsPbBr3 films in ambient air for large-area inorganic perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Laboratory for Deep Blue Fishery Engineering
Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-purity cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr3) perovskite films are expected to promote the commercial manufacture of inorganic perovskite solar cells (PSCs). We present here a spray-assisted deposition of high-quality CsPbBr3 films in ambient air by spin-coating a PbBr2 layer and sequentially spraying CsBr multilayer to simplify the film fabrication technology. Upon optimizing spray deposition at four cycles, a high-purity CsPbBr3 phase is prepared and a maximized power conversion efficiency of 5.68% is achieved for the carbon-based inorganic PSC device. Furthermore, molybdenum disulfide quantum dots with an intermediate energy level are set at CsPbBr3/carbon interface to markedly increase charge extraction and to suppress electron-hole recombination, arising from the perovskite grain boundary passivation. Finally, the optimal inorganic PSC yields a stabilized efficiency of 6.80% under one sun illumination. Using this spray-assisted deposition method, this CsPbBr3 solar cell with an active area of 1 cm2 achieves an efficiency of 4.12%. The photovoltaic performances are relatively stable even upon 80% relative humidity over 700 h without encapsulation.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have suffered a rushing development in recent few years, and an unprecedented power conversion efficiency (PCE) over 23% has been achieved employing organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite films as light harvesters [1–3]. Although great achievements have been obtained for these solar cells based on organic cations such as methylammonium (MA) and formamidinium (FA), the inherent poor-tolerances of MAPbX3 and FAPbX3 (X = I, Br, Cl) halides upon humidity and heat attacks are still challengeable for practical applications [4,5]. One of the promising solutions to this impasse is to replace organic cation with an inorganic cesium ion (Cs+) for inorganic cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr3) halide, which demonstrates high carrier mobility fabricated by a solution-processable technique [6,7]. To the best of our knowledge, an original PCE of 6.7% is reported on the inorganic PSC prototype with FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3/carbon configuration free of hole transporting layer (HTL). In this fashion, the significantly reduced production cost accelerates the commercialization of inorganic PSCs. On the other hand, the hydrophobic carbon back-electrode can effectively prevent perovskite layer being attacked by ambient atmosphere. Therefore, these devices are highlighted by extraordinary stability under high humidity and temperature conditions. Unfortunately, the sluggish solar-to-electrical conversion ability comparing to that of hybrid devices is a fatal factor for further development of all-inorganic solar cells. To address this issue, enormous efforts have been made on developing high-performance CsPbBr3 solar cells by suppressing the charge recombination and simplifying fabrication technologies to compensate this deficiency.

Perovskite-structured light-absorber is the heart of splitting electron-hole pairs under sunlight illumination, and many methodologies are developed to obtain high-quality perovskite films, including sequential deposition [8], vacuum evaporation [9], vapor-assisted deposition [10], spraying [11], solvent engineering [12], and additive engineering [13,14]. Among them, spraying technique has advantages over spin coating due to its ability to scale up for a large area solar cell, therefore it is always popular in industrial fabrication processes. In comparison with state-of-the-art spin-coating method, spraying deposition can markedly increase the utilization rate of the solar cell materials for arbitrary sizes and thickness. Using spraying deposition, a PCE as high as 11% has been demonstrated for CH3NH3PbI3-x Cl x PSCs [15]. However, there are no reports on all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cells assisted by this promising technique. According to our previous study, multi-step spin-coating method is preferred to realize high-quality CsPbBr3 film, in which sequential spin-coating increases the burden of commercialization of solar cells. To address this issue, we present here a simplified strategy to fabricate high-quality CsPbBr3 perovskite films by combining spin-coating and spraying processes. In detail, a PbBr2 layer is spin-coated onto the mesoscopic TiO2 (m-TiO2) surface and then CsBr multilayer is made by sequentially spraying CsBr solution onto PbBr2 film, realizing the phase conversion from CsPb2Br5 to CsPbBr3 and then Cs4PbBr6 by balancing PbBr2/CsBr ratio. Comparing to traditional spin-coating method, the spray-assisted technology can simplify the fabrication processes and provide a possibility to all-spraying processable strategy upon resolving the film quality of PbBr2 employing spraying method in the future.

There are serious charge recombination at CsPbBr3 (E VB = −5.6 eV)/carbon (WF = −5.0 eV) interface due to large interfacial energy difference and perovskite grain boundaries. Till now, charge extraction from perovskite layer to charge-transporting materials has attracted widespread interests for high-performance PSC platforms. Recently, two-dimensional graphene oxides and transition metal dichalcogenides nanomaterials have been used in organic solar cells and PSCs to modify the device performances because of their unique lamellar and electrical structures, resulting in high voltage and enhanced stability [16–20]. In order to further improve the PCE of CsPbBr3 device, in this work, molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) synthesized by a simple reflux method are employed to modify CsPbBr3/carbon interface by setting an intermediate energy level. The addition of few-layered MoS2 QDs can improve the film-forming capability and conductivity [21,22], more importantly, passivate the CsPbBr3 grain boundaries [23]. Through a spray-assisted technique and interfacial engineering, the optimal PSC yields a maximized PCE of 6.80% under one sun irradiation and an efficiency of 4.12% is achieved for the device with a large area of 1.0 cm2.

MoS2 QDs were synthesized according to the following procedures: In detail, 1 g of MoS2 powders were dissolved in 100 mL of N,N-dimethylbenzylamine (DMBA) under vigorous agitation for 30 min. Then the resultant mixture was suffered a sonication treatment at a power of 400 W for 4 h to exfoliate MoS2, and the resultant suspension was settled overnight without disturbance. The top dispersion was decanted into flask and vigorously stirred at 140 °C for 6 h. Finally, the suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to separate the sediments from supernatant. The light yellow MoS2 QDs supernatants were collected and dried in vacuum.

All the fabrication steps for inorganic PSC devices were conducted in ambient air atmosphere. Commercially available FTO glass was firstly etched by Zn powders and 2.0 M HCl aqueous solution for desirable patterns and then sequentially rinsed with acetone, ethanol and deionized water. The compact TiO2 (c-TiO2) layer was deposited on FTO substrate by spin-coating an ethanol solution of titanium isopropoxide (0.5 M) and diethanol amine (0.5 M) at 7000 rpm for 30 s and annealing in air at 500 °C for 2 h. The mesoporous TiO2 (m-TiO2) layer was then deposited by spin-coating a mixture of TiO2 paste (with average particle size of 20 nm) at 2000 rpm for 30 s and dried at 120 °C for 10 min, and annealed in air at 450 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the substrate was immersed in an aqueous solution of 0.04 M TiCl4 at 75 °C for 30 min, rinsed with water and ethanol, and then annealed at 450 °C for another 30 min. 1.0 M PbBr2 solution in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was spin-coated onto the m-TiO2 layer at 2000 rpm for 30 s, followed by drying at 80 °C for 30 min. After that, 100 μL of 0.07 M CsBr in methanol was sprayed on the PbBr2 layer in air at room temperature. The distance between the spray nozzle and the substrate was controlled at 10 cm. The substrate was then annealed at 250 °C for 10 min to crystallize the film in air. By repeatedly spraying CsBr solution and annealing for several times, high-quality CsPbBr3 perovskite films were thus obtained. The MoS2 buffer layer was deposited onto CsPbBr3 layer by spin coating MoS2 QD solution at 2000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, the carbon back-electrode was used to cover device by coating commercial carbon ink purchased from Shanghai MaterWin New Materials Co. with a doctor blade method and then heated at 70 °C for 10 min.

The photocurrent density (J)–voltage (V) curves of PSCs with an active area of 0.04 cm2 controlled by a black mask were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation under irradiation of simulated solar light from a 100 W xenon arc lamp in ambient atmosphere. The scan rate was controlled at 0.1 V s−1. The incident light intensity was controlled to 100 mW cm−2 (calibrated by using a standard silicon solar cell). The photovoltaic performances were repeatedly measured at least 30 times to eliminate experimental errors. The devices were stored under 80% RH in ambient air without encapsulation to test the long-term stability. The incident-photo-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was characterized by a power source (Newport 300 W xenon lamp, 66920) with a monochromator (Newport Cornerstone 260) in the 300–850 nm wavelength range at room temperature. Charge recombination in devices was evaluated by analyzing their J–V characteristics as a function of light intensity from 0.1 to 100 mW cm−2.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterizations and mapping images were performed on a SU8220 field emission microscope equipped with an SEM instrument to obtain top-view and cross-sectional morphologies. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, German Bruker Dimension Icon) was conducted at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, America FEI-Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN) was used to characterize the morphology of MoS2 QDs. The crystal structure of the obtained CsPbBr3 film was assessed by an X-ray diffraction (PHILIPSPW1800 diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation). The absorbance spectra of inorganic perovskite films were measured using a Meipuda UV-3200 spectrophotometer. The time-resolved PL measurement was carried out using time-resolved fluorescence (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FL) with excitation wavelength of 510 nm and the PL spectra of perovskite films were measured on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer under 385 nm wavelength excitation. UPS measurements were carried out using a Kratos AXIS ULTRA system with a helium discharge lamp, and with a concentric hemispherical analyzer for photoexcited electron detection.

The complete conversion of PbBr2 and CsBr into high-purity and large-size CsPbBr3 grains with compact stacking is significant for high-performance PSC devices. In current work, TiO2 and PbBr2 layers are deposited by a common spin-coating method owing to the inferior film quality employing spraying strategy, while CsBr multilayer is sequentially sprayed onto PbBr2 monolayer, as shown in Fig. 1 a. With developing advanced method to improve film quality of PbBr2 by controlling the crystallization process, the all-spraying processable route to fabricate solar cells can be realized in the future. The mesoscopic TiO2, PbBr2 and CsPbBr3 films are subjected to top-view SEM characterization. Obviously, the coverage of perovskite film at the 1st spraying deposition number (n) is low but it increases at higher n cycles. The PbBr2 layer is totally coverage by CsPbBr3 film with average grain size of 400–800 nm at n = 4. Although perovskite grains become larger at n ≥ 5, the pinholes from grain boundary will lead to the reduced charge collection in final solar cells. It can be found that there are obvious boundaries among grain particles, making it difficult to effectively collect charges from perovskite to TiO2 or to carbon layer and therefore increasing serious the photo-induced electrons and holes recombination. How to suppress this unwanted recombination is crucial to enhance the photovoltaic performance of solar cells. Fig. 1b presents a top-view SEM image of the perovskite layer at n = 4 after solution-processable coverage by MoS2 QDs, in which these boundaries are filled by hole-transporting MoS2 layer without changing the grain size (around 600 nm) of CsPbBr3. In this fashion, the holes excited from CsPbBr3 halides can be effectively extracted by MoS2 QDs, leaving electrons to electron-transporting layer. The passivation effect of MoS2 QDs calls for increased charge extraction and reduced interfacial charge recombination, therefore enhancing photovoltaic performances including short-circuit current density (J sc), open-circuit voltage (V oc), fill factor (FF) and PCE. To confirm the QDs distribution and chemical compositions, SEM mapping is scanned throughout the film. As presented in Fig. S1, Mo and S elements have homogeneous distributions over the film surface, confirming the homogeneous coverage of perovskite layer by MoS2 QDs. The laminated construction and illustrated diagram of a complete PSC device with FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/MoS2 QDs/carbon configuration are shown in Fig. 1c and d. Upon sunlight illumination from transparent FTO glass, the photons penetrating c-TiO2/m-TiO2 bi-layer are captured by CsPbBr3 to release electron-hole pairs, as described in Fig. 1e. Driven by local electric field, electrons flow from conduction band (CB) of CsPbBr3 (−3.3 eV) to TiO2 (−4.2 eV) and then to FTO (−4.6 eV), leaving holes to valence band (VB) of MoS2 (−5.4 eV) and carbon electrode (−5.0 eV). The corresponding characterizations of MoS2 QDs are discussed in the following section.

Fig. 2 a displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles of FTO/TiO2/perovskite films deposited at various CsBr cycles, and magnified XRD patterns at 2θ = 11–17° are shown in Fig. 2b to study the phase conversion mechanism. Obviously, a sharp peak at 2θ = 11.66° is found for PbBr2-rich tetragonal CsPb2Br5 phase at n = 1 [24], and the peak intensity for CsPbBr3 is relatively weak at 2θ = 15.25°. This means that only a small quantity of CsPbBr3 perovskite phase is formed by the one-step spraying owing to the insufficient CsBr amount in the film. Interestingly, the peak intensity for CsPb2Br5 structure reduces along with increasing spraying number until disappears at higher cycle number, while the orthorhombic CsPbBr3 phase becomes the majority and reaches to maximum at n = 4. Further increased cycle number allows the creation of trigonal Cs4PbBr6 phase because the detection at 2θ = 12.67° and 12.92° [25]. This study reveals a phase conversion from PbBr2-rich CsPb2Br5 (n ≤ 3) to perovskite-structured CsPbBr3 (n = 4) and further to CsBr-rich Cs4PbBr6 (n ≥ 5) by spraying CsBr multilayers sequentially, as shown in Fig. 2c, which is accordance with our previous report [26]. The presence of CsPb2Br5 and Cs4PbBr6 impurities are harmful to charge transfer at grain boundaries and long-term stability of CsPbBr3 film [27]. Through systematic study, high-purity CsPbBr3 film can be obtained with spray-assisted solution-processable technique by controlling one-layer PbBr2 and four-layer CsBr. The primary results provide new opportunities of making high-performance inorganic PSCs by multi-step spray-assisted method besides commonly used one-step and two-step technique, accelerating the large-scale production of solar cells.

One of the prerequisites for high-efficiency inorganic PSCs is to quickly extract photo-induced charges from CsPbBr3 layer, however, the energy difference as large as 0.6 eV at CsPbBr3/carbon interface will result in serious electron-hole recombination, not mention to the interstitial perovskite grain boundaries in the as-prepared perovskite films. To address these two issues, MoS2 QDs are used to optimize energy level distribution at perovskite/carbon interface for fast hole extraction and to passivate the grain boundaries by spin-coating MoS2 QD solution onto CsPbBr3 surface. TEM characterization in Fig. S2a shows a circular shape and homogeneous dispersion of MoS2 QDs with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.201 nm. High-resolution TEM image indicates a hexagonal structure [28], while they have a narrow particle size distribution (mainly 4–7 nm, Fig. S2c) by obeying a Gaussian mode. The determined thickness at 0.3–2.3 nm from AFM observation (Fig. S2b and d) suggests the as-prepared MoS2 QDs are composed of one or several layers [29]. To better understand the charge transfer paths within solar cells, UPS measurements are performed to demonstrate the VB and CB of MoS2 QDs, in which the work-function (Φ) of MoS2 QD is approximately 3.8 eV (Φ = 21.2–17.4 = 3.8 eV) and therefore the VB can be calculated to −5.4 eV, as shown in Fig. S3a and b. Assisting with the optical bandgap of MoS2 QDs (2.8 eV, see Fig. S3c), the CB of MoS2 QDs is assigned to be −2.6 eV. In this fashion, the photo-induced holes can be extracted by MoS2 QDs and then transferred to carbon electrode. The better energy level matching between CsPbBr3 and carbon reduces the charge recombination at the interface, leading to a large enhancement of V oc and PCE.

To evaluate the effects of spraying number and the MoS2 QDs interlayer on photovoltaic performances, the photo–current (J–V) curves of the various inorganic PSCs are measured (Fig. 3 a) and the corresponding parameters are summarized in Table 1 . An initial PCE of only 0.067% is achieved at n = 1, while it increases to maximum 5.68% at n = 4 and reduces at higher CsBr deposition cycles, which is mainly attributed to the phase conversion and therefore changing the light harvest ability (see Fig. 2). Upon modification with MoS2 QDs between CsPbBr3 and carbon electrode, the solar cell yields an efficiency as high as 6.80% under one sun illumination. The increased J sc from 5.73 to 6.55 mA cm−2 and V oc from 1.285 to 1.307 V indicate that the MoS2 QDs can modify the grain boundaries and energy level distribution, which have promoting effect in accelerating charge extraction and hindering electron-hole recombination at CsPbBr3/carbon interface. Furthermore, the J–V curves of the champion device are measured at a reverse and forward scan in order to investigate the hysteresis effect of all-inorganic PSCs with or without MoS2 QDs (Fig. S4), and the corresponding photovoltaic parameters of these perovskite devices are summarized in Table S1. It can be seen that there is a hysteresis in the resultant J–V curves for PSCs fabricated by spraying-assisted deposition, which could be related to the imperfect energy level alignment at TiO2/perovskite or perovskite/carbon interfaces and the existence of ubiquitous surface traps and defects on the surface of perovskite film [30]. It should be noted that the device with MoS2 QDs exhibits reduced hysteresis between the reverse and the forward scan comparing to that of the pristine device, mainly ascribing to the more efficient hole transport at perovskite/MoS2 QDs interface. Furthermore, 60 individual FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3/MoS2/carbon devices have a narrow distribution range of 5.25–7.25% with an average PCE of 6.50%, as shown in Fig. S5a. A little deviation suggests a good reproducibility of all-inorganic PSCs using the spray-assisted method reported here. To ensure the validity of this conclusion, the stable output curves of the MoS2 QDs-based device using maximum power point tracking model is performed in Fig. S5b, indicating a stable output feature within 200 s. The incident photo-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra of the best-performing solar cells with and without MoS2 QDs (Fig. 3b) to study the potential mechanism behind performance enhancement. The pristine all-inorganic PSC has a narrow absorption in a wavelength range of 325–550 nm with a maximum IPCE value of 75%, and an integrated current density of 5.74 mA cm−2 is obtained which is in accordance with the measured J–V result. Upon incorporating MoS2 QDs into the interface between perovskite and carbon electrode as hole-extracting and grain boundary passivation materials, the IPCE values are increased to maximum 85% without changing light-response window, which can be cross-checked by the absorbance spectra of CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/MoS2 films (Fig. S6). The integrated current density is also increased to 6.4 mA cm−2 which is comparable to 6.55 mA cm−2 for measured J sc output. These results suggest a boosting effect of MoS2 QDs in high-performance inorganic PSCs.

To highlight the charge transfer mechanism upon interfacial modification, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3 films with or without MoS2 QDs are recorded under excitation wavelength of 385 nm, as shown in Fig. 3c. A sharp peak centered at ∼530 nm assigning to CsPbBr3 is detected, while the peak is markedly quenched after introducing MoS2 QDs onto CsPbBr3 surface. Taking the charge recombination into consideration, the photo-induced holes can be extracted by MoS2 QDs and therefore suppressing the recombination of electrons in CB and holes in VB of CsPbBr3. Moreover, the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of pure perovskite and bilayer perovskite/MoS2 QDs thin films are also tested to cross-check quenching effect of MoS2 QDs toward perovskite film, as shown in Fig. 3d. The corresponding decay times are fitted and summarized in Table S2 according to a biexponential decay model. When a pure perovskite film is deposited on FTO substrate, shorter lifetime (τ1) and longer lifetime (τ2) mainly assigning to trap-mediated nonradiative recombination and radiative recombination are 0.43 ns and 3.01 ns, respectively [31]. Upon incorporating MoS2 QDs on the surface of CsPbBr3 film, both the values of τ1 and τ2 decrease, down to 0.36 ns and 2.11 ns According to previous study, τ1 is mainly dependent on the time of carrier extraction from the perovskite to the carrier transport layers [19]. In this fashion, reduced shorter time is preferred, arising from the decreased trap state and boosted charge transfer at interfaces, and this is consistent with the above-mentioned discussion. In the case of τ2, shorter time is mainly attributed to the fast charge extraction from CsPbBr3 film owing to the nearly unchanged crystal sizes as shown in Fig. 1. Therefore, both quenched PL intensity and reduced carrier lifetime confirm the promoting hole extraction from perovskite to MoS2 QDs and passivation effect at perovskite/MoS2 QDs interface [32,33]. To gain deep insights into the correlations, charge recombination in devices is evaluated by analyzing their J–V characteristics as a function of light intensity (I) as shown in Fig. 3e and f. According to J sc ∝ I α (α ≤ 1), α approaches unity when bimolecular radiative recombination is negligible [34,35]. A α value of 0.955 is recorded for pristine CsPbBr3 film based device, while it is increased to α = 0.985 for CsPbBr3/MoS2 QDs based device, suggesting that the bimolecular radiative recombination is markedly suppressed by setting an intermediate energy. In other words, the charge extraction ability is enhanced. Furthermore, according to V oc ∝ ɛkT/elnI, where k is the Boltzmann constant, T is absolute temperature, e refers to elementary charge, ɛ is related to trap-dominated recombination, the slop value is reduced from 1.798kT/e to 1.554kT/e after interfacial modification with MoS2 QDs [36–38]. This result means that MoS2 QDs have significantly suppressed the trap assisted recombination reactions and therefore increased photoelectric conversion efficiency. All of these advantages are mainly attributed to the passivation effect at the grain boundaries. Overall, the improved photovoltaic performances calls for interfacial modification of one or several layered MoS2 QDs in either/both extracting holes or/and passivating grain boundaries.

One of the challenging problems for commercial PSCs is the long-term stability in high humidity condition. To evaluate this performance, the all-inorganic PSCs without encapsulation are placed in 80% RH over 700 h, and the normalized photovoltaic parameters including PCE, J sc, V oc and FF are shown in Fig. S7. The MoS2 QDs tailored PSC device remains 97% of initial efficiency in comparison to 94% for pristine solar cell, while J sc, V oc and FF have almost no reduction in a so high humid air. This improved stability is mainly attributed to hydrophobic surface of carbon back-electrode and high humidity-resistance of inorganic CsPbBr3 crystals.

Large-scale PSCs at least 1 cm2 are also important for modeling commercial all-inorganic photovoltaics. To determine the relationship between solar cell size and performances, we use this spray-assisted technique to prepare MoS2 QDs tailored PSCs with active sizes ranging from 4 to 300 mm2. Fig. 4 a shows the J–V plots of these devices under one sun illumination and the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 2 . The PCE is 6.80% for a 0.04 cm2-device and reduces along with increasing cell area. Finally, it is determined to be 4.12% for 1 cm2-PSC (Fig. 4b). Using this method, the cell area can be further increased to 3 cm2 with a PCE of 2.33%. Owing to increased resistance of TiO2 films and therefore charge recombination, other parameters such as J sc, V oc and FF also have a reduction at large size. Fig. 4c shows the images of all-inorganic PSCs at different sizes, the CsPbBr3 films and carbon back-electrode have a homogenous coverage. In fact, the spray-assisted solution-processable method has a widespread application in making large-scale PSC devices by tuning preparation conditions although the current PCE is still lower than that of hybrid device or all-inorganic device based on other technology, for example, the distance between spray nozzle and the substrate, the speed of spray, etc. Further study on preparing PbBr2 and CsBr films with full spraying technique is still on progress.

In summary, we launch a spray-assisted solution-processable method to make CsPbBr3 films for all-inorganic PSC devices. By tuning spraying cycle of CsBr layer, the solar cell efficiency is maximized to be 5.68% under one sun illumination. The phase transformation process from PbBr2-riched CsPb2Br5 (n ≤ 3) to perovskite-structured CsPbBr3 (n = 4) and further to CsBr-riched Cs4PbBr6 (n ≥ 5) is studied to optimize preparation conditions. Using MoS2 QDs as interfacial modification materials, an intermediate energy level at CsPbBr3/carbon interface and passivating CsPbBr3 grain boundaries are simultaneously realized, markedly promoting hole extraction and cell efficiency. The optimized device achieves a maximum PCE as high as 6.80% for a 0.04 cm2 device and 4.12% for standard 1 cm2-scaled inorganic PSC. The successful realization of physical proof-of-concept spray-assisted method provides new opportunities of making large-scale perovskite-structured solar cells in a high efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503202 and 61774139) and Director Foundation from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (QNLM201702).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtener.2018.09.001.
